chunk_summary: This section discusses lifestyle interventions as first-line therapy for pediatric obesity, classification of obesity severity, and management of metabolic dysfunction-associated steatotic liver disease (MASLD) in children.
learning_objectives: Understand the classification of pediatric obesity using BMI percentiles.
Identify effective lifestyle interventions for managing pediatric obesity and MASLD.
Recognize when to refer pediatric patients to endocrinology or hepatology specialists.
key_facts: Obesity is classified as overweight (85th–<95th percentile), obesity (≥95th percentile), and severe obesity (≥120% of the 95th percentile or BMI ≥35 kg/m2).
Initial assessment should include blood pressure, sleep history, psychosocial screening, and evaluation for comorbidities like dyslipidemia, prediabetes, and liver disease.
Lifestyle interventions include reducing sugar-sweetened beverages, increasing fruit/vegetable intake, and reducing ultra-processed foods.
MASLD should be suspected in children with obesity and persistently elevated ALT or imaging evidence of steatosis.
Pharmacotherapy or bariatric referral is reserved for adolescents with severe obesity and significant comorbidities when lifestyle interventions fail.
reference_quote: Lifestyle intervention is first-line therapy for pediatric overweight and obesity and should be framed as treatment of a chronic cardiometabolic condition, not short-term 'dieting.'
